SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported by reported accuracy improvements on MR and SST and by ablation/targeted subset analyses, and the proposed KL-divergence margin regularizers and parameterizations are plausible and internally consistent. Mathematical apparatus appears straightforward (margin losses, class-specific shifts, word transforms) and no complex derivations are required, so no obvious mathematical errors are evident from the summary. Methodologically the paper compares to appropriate baselines (including tree-LSTMs) but omits details such as hyperparameter settings, code, and statistical significance testing, and it admits important limitations (no scope modeling for negation/intensifiers). Given these minor but relevant gaps, the technical claims are largely supported though some experimental/reporting shortcomings prevent a perfect score. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing improved accuracy on MR and SST and by ablation/targeted-subset analyses demonstrating capture of negation/intensifier effects. The proposed KL-divergence margin regularizers and parameterizations are plausible and no mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. Experimental methodology is generally sound with strong baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL), but the summary lacks details on hyperparameter tuning and statistical significance testing. The authors acknowledge key limitations (no scope modeling, limited coverage of negation/intensity), so conclusions are reasonably justified though some experimental reporting details are missing.

The authors acknowledge key limitations (no scope modeling, limited coverage of negation/intensity), so conclusions are reasonably justified though some experimental reporting details are missing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a distinct methodological perspective—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that constrain sentiment-distribution drift via KL-divergence margins—and integrates sentiment lexicons, negation and intensity handling into LSTM/Bi-LSTM, which goes beyond standard LSTM/Tree-LSTM/CNN baselines. Its parameterization of sentiment shifts with class-specific shifting distributions and word-specific transform matrices and the bidirectional treatment (min distance of transformed forward/backward distributions) are non-trivial technical extensions that clearly differentiate the work from prior models like NCSL and Tree-LSTM. At the same time, because it builds on well-studied sequence models and lexicon-based ideas rather than a wholly new paradigm, the contribution is notable but somewhat incremental rather than radically novel. | B(3): The paper proposes a concrete new mechanism—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) applied via KL-divergence margin losses and parameterized by class-specific shifting distributions and word-specific transform matrices—integrated into LSTM/Bi-LSTM. Sentiment classification is a well-studied problem, and while the work clearly differentiates itself by combining lexicons, negation and intensity modeling with distributional regularization, the approach is an incremental, non-trivial extension of existing LSTM and lexicon-based methods rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An expert would find the specific regularizer designs and their empirical analysis informative but not highly surprising.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a distinct methodological perspective—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that constrain sentiment-distribution drift via KL-divergence margins—and integrates sentiment lexicons, negation and intensity handling into LSTM/Bi-LSTM, which goes beyond standard LSTM/Tree-LSTM/CNN baselines. Its parameterization of sentiment shifts with class-specific shifting distributions and word-specific transform matrices and the bidirectional treatment (min distance of transformed forward/backward distributions) are non-trivial technical extensions that clearly differentiate the work from prior models like NCSL and Tree-LSTM. At the same time, because it builds on well-studied sequence models and lexicon-based ideas rather than a wholly new paradigm, the contribution is notable but somewhat incremental rather than radically novel.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a concrete new mechanism—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) applied via KL-divergence margin losses and parameterized by class-specific shifting distributions and word-specific transform matrices—integrated into LSTM/Bi-LSTM. Sentiment classification is a well-studied problem, and while the work clearly differentiates itself by combining lexicons, negation and intensity modeling with distributional regularization, the approach is an incremental, non-trivial extension of existing LSTM and lexicon-based methods rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. An expert would find the specific regularizer designs and their empirical analysis informative but not highly surprising.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs experiments on two standard benchmarks (MR and SST) and compares against a broad set of baselines, and includes ablation and targeted-subset experiments that demonstrate the effect of the linguistic regularizers. Related work appears to be covered thoroughly, and the authors explicitly discuss failure cases and limitations (scope of negation/intensifiers, low frequency of such words, and specific failure examples). However, reproducibility is limited because code is not mentioned and hyperparameters are not reported, and the empirical evaluation is confined to two datasets which may understate some claims about negation/intensity handling. Overall the experimental analysis is solid but has minor gaps in reproducibility and dataset breadth. | B(4): The paper runs comparisons against strong baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, NCSL) on two standard datasets (MR, SST) and reports accuracy improvements, and includes ablation and targeted-subset experiments that demonstrate the linguistic regularizers capture negation/intensifier effects. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the authors discuss limitations and failure cases (scope not modeled, low prevalence of negation/intensity, some incorrect shifts). However, reproducibility is weak—no code or hyperparameters are reported—and evaluation is limited to two datasets with relatively few negation/intensifier instances, leaving some gaps in experimental comprehensiveness.

Overall the experimental analysis is solid but has minor gaps in reproducibility and dataset breadth.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, DAN, NCSL) on standard datasets (MR, SST) using accuracy, and includes ablation and targeted analyses to distinguish its contributions. However, reproducibility/fairness information is incomplete (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and it is not clearly stated whether prior methods sometimes perform comparably or better. Related work coverage is described as thorough, so the omissions are minor rather than fundamental. | B(4): The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN/CNN-Tensor, DAN, NCSL) on standard datasets (MR and SST) using the same accuracy metric, and includes ablation and targeted subset experiments to analyze differences. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the methodological differences and linguistic effects are explicitly evaluated. However, hyperparameters and code are not reported and the summary does not state whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive on reproducibility/details.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses sentence-level sentiment classification using linguistically informed regularizers on LSTM/Bi-LSTM, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It targets the right audience by combining neural sequence models with lexicon-based linguistic signals (sentiment, negation, intensity) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) against appropriate baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL). The problem formulation and experimental setup (accuracy on established datasets, ablation/targeted subset analyses) match expectations for the venue, and the work is positioned with thorough related work coverage. Minor reporting gaps (e.g., no hyperparameter/code mention) do not change its clear relevance to the conference community. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (sentence-level sentiment classification) and proposes linguistically motivated regularizers integrated with LSTM/Bi-LSTM, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard datasets (MR, SST), compares to appropriate baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL) and evaluates with common metrics (accuracy), indicating it targets the right audience and uses expected evaluation standards. The problem formulation (regularizing distributional sentiment shifts with KL-margin losses and word/class-specific transforms) and thorough related-work discussion position the paper well within the NLP/ML research community.

It targets the right audience by combining neural sequence models with lexicon-based linguistic signals (sentiment, negation, intensity) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) against appropriate baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL). The problem formulation and experimental setup (accuracy on established datasets, ablation/targeted subset analyses) match expectations for the venue, and the work is positioned with thorough related work coverage. reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (sentence-level sentiment classification) and proposes linguistically motivated regularizers integrated with LSTM/Bi-LSTM, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard datasets (MR, SST), compares to appropriate baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN, NCSL) and evaluates with common metrics (accuracy), indicating it targets the right audience and uses expected evaluation standards. The problem formulation (regularizing distributional sentiment shifts with KL-margin losses and word/class-specific transforms) and thorough related-work discussion position the paper well within the NLP/ML research community.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper targets an important problem—making sequence models for sentence-level sentiment classification more linguistically informed—by proposing four regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that incorporate lexicons, negation and intensity and showing improved accuracy on MR and SST. The approach is practically useful and could influence researchers to combine lexicon-based constraints with neural sequence encoders, but the impact is moderate because the gains appear incremental, negation/intensity effects are limited in the datasets (only ~14%/23% of sentences), and the model does not handle modification scope or deeper syntactic/semantic structure. Overall, the work is a solid, reusable contribution that opens some follow-up directions but is unlikely to be transformative across NLP. | B(3): The paper targets the important task of sentence-level sentiment classification and introduces linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that improve accuracy on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) and produce interpretable sentiment-shift behaviors. However, the contribution is incremental rather than transformative: negation/intensity regularizers have limited effect (only 14%/23% of sentences contain such cues) and the model does not model modification scope, reducing its generality. The work is likely to be useful and to inspire follow-up research on incorporating lexical and syntactic priors into neural models, but it is unlikely to fundamentally change the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important problem—making sequence models for sentence-level sentiment classification more linguistically informed—by proposing four regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that incorporate lexicons, negation and intensity and showing improved accuracy on MR and SST. The approach is practically useful and could influence researchers to combine lexicon-based constraints with neural sequence encoders, but the impact is moderate because the gains appear incremental, negation/intensity effects are limited in the datasets (only ~14%/23% of sentences), and the model does not handle modification scope or deeper syntactic/semantic structure. Overall, the work is a solid, reusable contribution that opens some follow-up directions but is unlikely to be transformative across NLP.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper targets the important task of sentence-level sentiment classification and introduces linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that improve accuracy on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) and produce interpretable sentiment-shift behaviors. However, the contribution is incremental rather than transformative: negation/intensity regularizers have limited effect (only 14%/23% of sentences contain such cues) and the model does not model modification scope, reducing its generality. The work is likely to be useful and to inspire follow-up research on incorporating lexical and syntactic priors into neural models, but it is unlikely to fundamentally change the field.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (the four linguistic regularizers: NSR, SR, NR, IR, plus the KL-divergence margin losses and parameterizations). Notation and methodological descriptions (class-specific shifting distributions, word-specific transform matrices) are presented consistently and the ablation/targeted-subset experiments effectively illustrate linguistic effects. Minor presentation gaps remain—e.g., hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned—which slightly reduce completeness but do not substantially hinder clarity. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM and four linguistic regularizers). Notation and methodology are described clearly enough to convey how KL-divergence based margin losses and class-/word-specific shift parameters are used, and experiments (MR, SST) with ablations and targeted subsets effectively illustrate the claims. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough; minor presentation gaps (e.g., hyperparameters and code not mentioned) prevent an exceptional rating but do not undermine overall clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (the four linguistic regularizers: NSR, SR, NR, IR, plus the KL-divergence margin losses and parameterizations). Notation and methodological descriptions (class-specific shifting distributions, word-specific transform matrices) are presented consistently and the ablation/targeted-subset experiments effectively illustrate linguistic effects. reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM and four linguistic regularizers). Notation and methodology are described clearly enough to convey how KL-divergence based margin losses and class-/word-specific shift parameters are used, and experiments (MR, SST) with ablations and targeted subsets effectively illustrate the claims. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage thorough; minor presentation gaps (e.g., hyperparameters and code not mentioned) prevent an exceptional rating but do not undermine overall clarity.

main_claims: Propose a linguistically regularized LSTM (LR-LSTM / LR-Bi-LSTM) that incorporates sentiment lexicons, negation words, and intensity words to improve sentence-level sentiment classification.

Introduce four linguistic regularizers—Non-Sentiment Regularizer (NSR), Sentiment Regularizer (SR), Negation Regularizer (NR), and Intensity Regularizer (IR)—that constrain sentiment distribution drift between adjacent positions using KL-divergence margin losses.

Parameterize sentiment shifts with class-specific shifting distributions and word-specific transformation matrices, and apply the regularizers to bidirectional LSTM to leverage both left and right contexts.

Demonstrate improved accuracy on Movie Review (MR) and Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SST) and show the model captures linguistic effects of negation and intensifiers through ablation and targeted subset experiments.
_summary.methodology: Augment LSTM/Bi-LSTM with linguistic regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) that impose KL-divergence based margin losses on predicted sentiment distributions; sentiment shifts are modeled by class-specific shifting distributions and word-specific transform matrices, and in Bi-LSTM the model uses transformed forward/backward distributions and takes the minimum distance for regularization.
limitations: The model does not consider the modification span (scope) of negation and intensity words.
Negation/intensity effects depend on syntax and semantics of the modified text, which the sequence LSTM encoding does not explicitly model.
Negation and intensity regularizers are less impactful partly because only 14% of sentences contain negation words and 23% contain intensity words in the test datasets.
The model sometimes fails to shift sentiment correctly with certain intensifiers (example failures reported).
